title: Winter feeding livestock from round bales
subtitle: How to set up and manage hay or straw round bale feeding for sheep, cattle, and horses.
excerpt: Feeding from round bales is practical at scale but produces significant waste if not managed well. The method (ring feeder, net, or open access) determines how much ends up eaten versus trampled.
sourceNotes: AHDB, winter feeding and forage management for cattle and sheep. SAC Consulting, round bale feeding guidance. NADIS, sheep nutrition in winter factsheet.

content.text: Round bale hay wastage without a feeder can reach 30 to 45 per cent of the bale. Sheep and cattle pull hay out and trample it, then refuse to eat the soiled material. A
reduces this to 5 to 15 per cent in most situations.

Remove the net wrap or string from the bale before placing the ring feeder. Standing the bale on its flat end in a ring feeder can be done with a loader or by two people rolling the bale into position. Ensure the ring is stable before releasing it; a rolling bale in a field is a hazard. Space one ring per 20 to 25 sheep or per 6 to 8 cows, so all animals can access the bale without dominant animals monopolising it.

For horses particularly, a bale net or large haybag allows a slow feed rate from a round bale. The net keeps the bale contained as it is consumed, reducing trampling. Bale nets for horses have small mesh to slow feeding; for cattle or sheep, large mesh or ring feeders are more appropriate.

Open access (bale placed in the field without a feeder) is the most wasteful method and should only be used when no feeder is available. To reduce waste, place the bale in a corner where wind does not blow hay away and where animals approach from one direction only. Move the bale location between placements to avoid heavy poaching in one spot.

Sheep and goats grazing beneath a ring feeder can ingest worm eggs deposited in droppings that fall there. Move the ring feeder to a new position each time a new bale is started to prevent concentration of contaminated ground in one spot. Do not allow sheep to eat hay that has fallen and been lying on wet, contaminated ground.
